MORNING.TITLE: Morning
MORNING.TEXT_1: On a warm summer morning in the city, people are waking up and getting ready for their day. The Sun is already shining brightly.

VEHICLE_TYPES.TITLE: Vehicle Types
VEHICLE_TYPES.TEXT_1: People use many different types of vehicles to get to where they are going - cars, buses, motorcycles, scooters, and bicycles. Most of these vehicles burn gasoline or natural gas, called fossil fuels, to make them go.

AIR_POLLUTION.TITLE: Air Pollution
AIR_POLLUTION.TEXT_1: Most of the gases we’re adding to the air, from things like burning fossil fuels or using industrial chemicals, are considered air pollution.
AIR_POLLUTION.TEXT_2: These gases make the air less clean, which is bad for our health.

HEALTH_IMPACTS.TITLE: Health Impacts
HEALTH_IMPACTS.TEXT_1: Air pollution can damage our lungs, and harm plants and animals, too.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.LUNGS_BEFORE: Healthy lungs have wide open pathways.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.LUNGS_AFTER: Ozone pollution narrows airways and makes it difficult to breathe.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.PLANTS_BEFORE: Healthy plants exchange gases with the environment through tiny openings in their leaves.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.PLANTS_AFTER: Ozone pollution damages leaves and reduces growth of plants and crops.

SUNNY_DAY.TITLE: Sunny Day
SUNNY_DAY.TEXT_1: But on a warm sunny day when certain types of air pollution are already in the atmosphere, something called ground-level ozone can form.

AIR.TITLE: Air
AIR.NARRATIVE: Can you guess what's in the air around us?
AIR.POST_CHOICE_TEXT: The air all around us — which is called the atmosphere — is mostly made up of nitrogen and oxygen and small amounts of other gases.

NEARBY_FACTORIES.TITLE: Nearby Factories
NEARBY_FACTORIES.TEXT_1:
Nearby factories also burn fossil fuels to make electricity that we use in our homes, schools,
and workplaces.
Factories and vehicles also release solid particles of dust, carbon soot, and smoke that are so tiny they can float in the air for a long time.

FUEL_SOURCES.TITLE: Fuel Sources
FUEL_SOURCES.TEXT_1: [PLACEHOLDER TEXT] Click the fossil fuel source to learn more about where they are found!
FUEL_SOURCES.OIL_BUBBLE: [PLACEHOLDER] Oil is extracted from underground reservoirs
FUEL_SOURCES.COAL_BUBBLE: [PLACEHOLDER] Coal is found in deposits within sedimentary rock layers

BURNING_FUELS.TITLE: Burning Fuel
BURNING_FUELS.TEXT_1: Burning fossil fuels releases invisible gases into the air — carbon dioxide, nitrogen dioxide, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and other gases, too. Factories and vehicles also release solid particles of dust, carbon soot, and smoke that are so tiny they can float in the air for a long time.
BURNING_FUELS.FACTORY_BUBBLE: We burn fossil fuels to create energy for the city!
BURNING_FUELS.CAR_BUBBLE: Engines burn gasoline to make cars move!

GATHER_INGREDIENTS.TITLE: Gathering Ingredients
GATHER_INGREDIENTS.INGREDIENT_EXPLAINATION:
Drag the “ingredients” needed to make ground level ozone to the smog cloud: 
 • VOCs from car exhaust pipes, paint, or other industrial products 
 • NO2 from car exhaust pipes and from factory emissions
GATHER_INGREDIENTS.INSTRUCTIONS_1: Great! Now click on the Sun to add the final ingredient: energy from the Sun.

OZONE_MOLECULE.TITLE: How Ozone Forms
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_1: Sunlight starts a chain of chemical reactions that causes ozone to form. Tap the Sun to start the reaction.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_2: Energy from the Sun reaches a NO2 molecule. Tap the NO₂ molecule.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_3: The sunlight causes an oxygen atom to break off from the NO₂ molecule. Tap the free oxygen atom.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_4: When that free oxygen atom bumps into an O₂ molecule it forms ozone (O₃). Tap the ozone molecule if you’d like to see the reaction again.

UPPER_OZONE.TITLE: Upper Atmosphere Ozone
UPPER_OZONE.TEXT_1: Now way up high in the atmosphere, ozone is naturally there in the ozone layer. Ozone up in the stratosphere is actually really helpful. It helps block UV rays from the Sun that can cause sunburn.

AREA.PROMPT: Ozone pollution is worse in certain areas. Can you guess where this happens?
AREA.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse in urban areas!
AREA.INCORRECT: Actually, no! There is less ozone pollution in rural areas!

SEASON.PROMPT: Another factor that influences ozone pollution is time of year. When might pollution be worse?
SEASON.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse in the hot and sunny months!
SEASON.INCORRECT: Actually, no! Cool and dark months will have less ozone pollution!

CARS_FACTORIES.PROMPT: Density of cars and factories also makes an impact since these emit the ingredients of ozone. Knowing this, where might pollution be worse?
CARS_FACTORIES.CHOICE_LOTS: Areas with lots of cars and factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.CHOICE_FEW: Areas with fewer cars or factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse where there are many cars and/or factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.INCORRECT: Actually, no! Cars and factories produce the ingredients for ozone, so pollution is worse where there are more of them!

SOLUTIONS.TITLE: Solutions
SOLUTIONS.PROMPT: There are many things we can do to reduce ozone pollution. Tap each scenario and choose what you think will reduce pollution.
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION: Nice work! You've chosen {{count}} actions to take to help reduce ozone pollution.
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_PERFECT: Fantastic! You chose {{correctCount}} helpful actions and didn't fall for any tricks!
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_MIXED: Good job finding {{correctCount}} helpful actions! However, {{incorrectCount}} of your choices would actually make pollution worse.
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_ALL_INCORRECT: Oh no! Those choices would actually create more ozone pollution. Let's be careful!

LOW_POLLUTION_VEHICLE.TITLE: Choose a low-pollution vehicle.
LOW_POLLUTION_VEHICLE.DESC: Fuel efficient cars and electric cars help keep air quality good.
LOW_POLLUTION_VEHICLE.FEEDBACK: Fuel-efficient and electric vehicles release far fewer ozone-forming chemicals into the air than older, less efficient vehicles.

DRIVE_EVERYWHERE.TITLE: Drive everywhere.
DRIVE_EVERYWHERE.DESC: Take your personal car for every trip, even short errands.
DRIVE_EVERYWHERE.FEEDBACK: Every car trip adds volatile organic compounds and nitrogen oxides to the air, which cook together to form ground-level ozone.

RENEWABLE_ENERGY.TITLE: Choose renewable energy.
RENEWABLE_ENERGY.DESC: Wind, solar, and other renewables make electricity without air pollution.
RENEWABLE_ENERGY.FEEDBACK: Solar and wind power generate electricity without burning fossil fuels, meaning zero ozone ingredients are emitted from power plants.

FOSSIL_FUELS.TITLE: Stick with fossil fuels.
FOSSIL_FUELS.DESC: Rely on coal and gas power plants since they are already built.
FOSSIL_FUELS.FEEDBACK: Coal and gas power plants are major sources of nitrogen oxides, a key ingredient in ground-level ozone formation.

LOW_VOC_PRODUCTS.TITLE: Choose low VOC products.
LOW_VOC_PRODUCTS.DESC: Choose paints, adhesives, household cleaners, and other products that have low or no VOCs.
LOW_VOC_PRODUCTS.FEEDBACK: Low-VOC paints and cleaners release fewer chemicals into the air as they dry, depriving the atmosphere of the 'V' in the ozone recipe.

HIGH_VOC_PRODUCTS.TITLE: Use standard products.
HIGH_VOC_PRODUCTS.DESC: Don't worry about the chemicals in your everyday paints and household cleaners.
HIGH_VOC_PRODUCTS.FEEDBACK: Standard household chemicals and paints evaporate volatile organic compounds (VOCs) right into the air around your home.

LESS_ENERGY.TITLE: Use less energy.
LESS_ENERGY.DESC: This will reduce air pollution if your energy comes from gas or coal and save money.
LESS_ENERGY.FEEDBACK: Using less electricity means power plants don't have to burn as much fuel, cutting down the pollution at the source.

LEAVE_LIGHTS_ON.TITLE: Leave lights on.
LEAVE_LIGHTS_ON.DESC: Keep all your lights and appliances running all day to save time.
LEAVE_LIGHTS_ON.FEEDBACK: Wasting electricity forces power plants to run harder and burn more fossil fuels, pumping more pollution into our skies.

FORGO_FIRE.TITLE: Forgo the fire.
FORGO_FIRE.DESC: On days when air quality is poor, avoid using your wood fireplace or wood stove if you can.
FORGO_FIRE.FEEDBACK: Choosing not to burn wood keeps particulate matter and volatile organic compounds out of the atmosphere.

BURN_WOOD.TITLE: Have a wood fire.
BURN_WOOD.DESC: Burn wood in your fireplace or fire pit even on poor air quality days.
BURN_WOOD.FEEDBACK: Wood smoke is full of harmful particulates and chemicals that worsen air quality and contribute to smog.

COMPOST_YARD_WASTE.TITLE: Compost yard waste.
COMPOST_YARD_WASTE.DESC: Avoid burning leaves, branches, and other plant trimmings by composting them.
COMPOST_YARD_WASTE.FEEDBACK: Composting breaks down leaves and branches naturally and safely, returning nutrients to the soil instead of pollution to the air.

BURN_TRASH.TITLE: Burn your trash.
BURN_TRASH.DESC: Get rid of waste quickly by burning it in your backyard.
BURN_TRASH.FEEDBACK: Burning trash and leaves releases a massive, concentrated plume of toxic chemicals and ozone precursors directly into your neighborhood.

MOW_EVENING.TITLE: Mow in the evening.
MOW_EVENING.DESC: This helps prevent ozone formation. Or use an electric or manual mower.
MOW_EVENING.FEEDBACK: Mowing when it's cooler means the engine emissions don't have the intense sunlight needed to bake into ozone.

MOW_MORNING.TITLE: Mow in the morning.
MOW_MORNING.DESC: Mow your lawn early in the day when it's cool outside.
MOW_MORNING.FEEDBACK: Gas-powered mowers lack pollution controls. Running them in the morning provides a fresh batch of chemicals right as the sun starts baking the air.

REFUEL_EVENING.TITLE: Refuel in the evening.
REFUEL_EVENING.DESC: At gas stations, gasoline vapors get into the air, which help make ozone on hot, sunny days.
REFUEL_EVENING.FEEDBACK: Pumping gas in the evening means those escaped gasoline fumes won't have hot sunlight to react with right away.

REFUEL_DAY.TITLE: Refuel during the day.
REFUEL_DAY.DESC: Fill up your gas tank during the hottest part of the sunny afternoon.
REFUEL_DAY.FEEDBACK: When you pump gas in the heat of the day, gasoline vapors escape and immediately start cooking in the sun to form ozone.

WALK_BIKE_BUS.TITLE: Walk, bike, or take the bus.
WALK_BIKE_BUS.DESC: These are low-cost ways to get from place to place and combine errands for efficiency.
WALK_BIKE_BUS.FEEDBACK: Sharing a ride or using human power removes a tailpipe from the road entirely, making a huge difference in local air quality.

IDLE_ENGINE.TITLE: Leave the engine running.
IDLE_ENGINE.DESC: Keep your car running while waiting to keep the engine warm.
IDLE_ENGINE.FEEDBACK: An idling engine gets zero miles per gallon while continuously spewing out pollution directly into the air.
